texts.full_text: o with the solution. Some other useful info: Windows 10 Git v2.18.0.windows.1 Shell: GNU bash, version 4.4.19(2)-release (x86_64-pc-msys) Is there a way to add git branch auto completion to a custom git extension command? An interesting related question for Linux: custom git command autocompletion. A: Using an alias Let's assume a minimal version of your script, something like #!/usr/bin/env bash git diff "$1" This is executable and somewhere in your $PATH. Let's call it gitreview. To get an alias for it, you add it like this in your .gitconfig: [alias] review = "!f() { gitreview \"$@\"; }; f" This gets you completion for review when you enter git. Now, to make it behave the same as git checkout, you can use a null command, like this: review = "!f() { : git checkout ; gitreview \"$@\"; }; f" and it'll complete the same as git checkout! Notice that the blank between checkout and ; is required. This is mentioned in the comments of git-completion.bash: # If you use complex aliases of form '!f()

texts.full_text: value of property in order to assess property taxes in Marion County, Mississippi. The Assessor’s Office is an important source for tax records. The Assessor’s Office maintains current data on the value of all property in the jurisdiction, including ownership information, maps of property lines, and Marion County property tax records. Many Assessor’s Offices also provide online access to property assessments and tax records. Marion County Tax Assessor Columbia MS 250 Broad Street 39429 601-736-8256 Marion County Recorders of Deeds maintain multiple kinds of records, particularly documents related to property and property taxes, and these records are available to the public in Marion County, Mississippi. The Recorder of Deeds can be an important source for tax records. The Recorder of Deeds keeps documents on land transactions, including deeds, mortgages, easements, Marion County tax liens, and other tax records. Many Recorders of Deeds provide online databases to access tax record information. Marion County Recorders of Deeds Columbia, MS 39429 601-736-8256 In addition to the Assessor’s Office and the Recorder of Deeds, Marion County, Mississippi, also has other offices and resources that can provide information on tax records: ### Marion County Treasurer The Treasurer's Office is responsible for collecting property taxes and maintaining records of payments. They can provide detailed information about tax bills, payment history, and any delinquencies. - Address: 250 Broad Street, Columbia, MS 39429 - Phone: 601-736-8256 ### Marion County Clerk The Clerk's Office often maintains a variety of public records, including those related to property taxes. They can provide copies of tax bills, payment records, and other relevant documents. - Address: 250 Broad Street, Columbia, MS 39429 - Phone: 601-736-8256 ### Online Resources Many of these offices offer online access to tax records. You can typically find this information on the official Marion County website or through specific department websites. - Marion County Website: [www.marioncounty.ms.gov](http://www.marioncounty.ms.gov) By visiting these resources, you can obtain comprehensive and up-to-date information about

texts.preview_tail: necessary to view Tagore as “a holistic artist”. He also referred to Tagore’s “remarkable contribution to the fine arts” and how he could transform a piece of manuscript into a work of art by blocking
texts.full_text: he travelled to London in 1912, where Yeats discovered his poetry and was “very moved” by it. “There was an innocence, a simplicity and a spontaneity in [the poems],” said Som. “They weren’t translations, but poetry reborn … expressions that came from the heart.” She said that Tagore often held London soirées, where Yeats would read and Tagore would sing. “To the reserved English, these poems touched them profoundly,” she said. Padel went on to explain that Yeats and Tagore were two poets who were “interested in the singing form and in taking their society forward.” They were both aware that their society needed to undergo change and they had “fantastic metaphors built into their songs”. Yeats, she said, saw his own work mirrored in Tagore – he saw “a kind of high theatricality that was genuine.” He looked for spiritual intellect and seemed to have found a similar soul in this Indian writer. There was, Padel added, an ongoing ‘battle’ between the modernists (e.g. Pound and Elliott) and Yeats about who was more true and emotionally genuine in their work. Indeed, as Fraser later pointed out, both Yeats and Tagore were “romantics and modern, rather than being modernists”. “Both of them felt that education and colour began with the mother tongue,” she said. “For them, that was English and Bengali. Both of them also bring the ordinary person into literature.” With Tagore, she said that the Western population had in him both a mystic and a pragmatist. However, what they wanted was “only mystics”. “I think that’s why people fell away from him,” she said. However, she pointed out that both Yeats and Tagore have remained very much relevant even today, adding that “they both never lost their faith in humanity”. In the question and answer session which followed, Sen said it was necessary to view Tagore as “a holistic artist”. He also referred to Tagore’s “remarkable contribution to the fine arts” and how he could transform a piece of manuscript into a work of art by blocking

texts.full_text: urred costs due to delays beyond those already compensated under the contract's force account provisions. Yates argues on appeal that the ASBCA erred in its decision, specifically regarding: 1. The applicability of the force account provisions. 2. Whether Yates was entitled to equitable adjustments for government-caused delays and cost overruns. 3. The sufficiency of Yates' documentation to support claimed costs beyond those covered by the contract's terms. The Federal Circuit reviews factual findings of the ASBCA under a clearly erroneous standard, while legal questions are reviewed de novo. After reviewing the record, briefs, and arguments presented, we affirm the decision of the Armed Services Board of Contract Appeals. The board did not err in its interpretation of the force account provisions or in finding that Yates failed to establish sufficient evidence
